title: Warren accuses AI firms of using trade deals to escape oversight
excerpt: Senator Elizabeth Warren has accused the biggest AI companies of trying to write their own regulatory ceiling into a trade agreement, using the machinery of US trade policy to lock in limits on oversight that Congress has so far declined to grant them. The accusation, reported by Reuters on July 23, extends a fight the Massachusetts Democrat […]
content_html:
Senator Elizabeth Warren has accused the biggest AI companies of trying to write their own regulatory ceiling into a trade agreement, using the machinery of US trade policy to lock in limits on oversight that Congress has so far declined to grant them.

The accusation, reported by Reuters on July 23, extends a fight the Massachusetts Democrat has waged for more than a year.

Her argument is that Big Tech is quietly turning contested domestic questions into trade concessions, the same logic behind the trade probe into EU tech rules that lawmakers pushed the administration to open.

The mechanism she points to is a clause, not a headline. A run of recent US bilateral deals, catalogued in a Center for Strategic and International Studies analysis of what it calls a “containment doctrine”, carry near-identical “non-discrimination” language that bars partners from measures that “discriminate against U.S. digital services”.

The 💜 of EU tech

The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now!

That wording is broad enough to reach the rules the industry has spent two years trying to blunt: the EU’s Digital Markets Act and Digital Services Act, and the patchwork of state AI statutes that Washington has separately tried to preempt at home. A rule that survives a state legislature can still be challenged as a trade barrier abroad.

Warren has made versions of this case straight to the administration. In a May 2025 letter to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, and trade representative Jamieson Greer, she wrote that “Big Tech firms have long sought to use trade deals to undermine pro-consumer, pro-competition policies”.

That letter named Apple, Amazon, Meta, and Google as funders of the industry groups pressing the US Trade Representative to treat foreign digital rules as barriers.

A follow-up in March 2026, prompted by the volume of sexual deepfakes generated with Elon Musk’s Grok, asked whether US negotiators had leaned on other countries to drop content-moderation laws outright.

In that letter she pointed to the administration’s handling of the EU’s Digital Services Act, which US officials have described as “overseas extortion”, alongside stalled talks over the UK’s Online Safety Act and France’s SREN law, which stiffens penalties for sexual deepfakes.

The through-line, she argued, is that content and competition rules keep surfacing at the negotiating table rather than in open rulemaking, where they can be debated and voted on.

The firms’ side of this is less a rebuttal than a reframing. The industry has argued consistently that measures like the DMA are protectionism dressed as consumer protection, discriminatory by design and therefore fair game for a trade complaint, an argument the same companies made while they bankrolled the campaign against AI rules at home.

The administration has been willing to back that reading with leverage, at one point threatening 100% tariffs over digital services taxes.

The CSIS analysis notes the tactic works unevenly: smaller economies such as Guatemala and Cambodia have signed accords carrying the restrictive clauses, while larger markets have held out.

The domestic side of the fight runs in parallel. Washington has separately weighed a federal standard that would override state AI statutes, and the industry has spent months girding to block those state rules, so a trade clause that reaches the same laws from outside the country would hand the effort a second lever.

What Warren cannot easily do is force the text into daylight. Trade negotiations are conducted with limited transparency, and she has co-sponsored legislation to require that deal text be published before Congress grants fast-track authority, so far without success.

Without the text, she has argued, neither lawmakers nor the public can judge what has actually been promised.

Whether the July charge lands any differently is unclear. Reuters reported the accusation; the companies named in her earlier letters have not addressed it publicly, and USTR has not answered the specific claim.

For now it is one senator’s reading of a clause most voters will never see, which is close to the point she is making.

The accusation, reported by Reuters on July 23, extends a fight the Massachusetts Democrat has waged for more than a year. Her argument is that Big Tech is quietly turning contested domestic questions into trade concessions, the same logic behind the trade probe into EU tech rules that lawmakers pushed the administration to open. The mechanism she points to is a clause, not a headline. A run of recent US bilateral deals, catalogued in a Center for Strategic and International Studies analysis of what it calls a “containment doctrine”, carry near-identical “non-discrimination” language that bars partners from measures that “discriminate against U.S. digital services”. Sign up now!That wording is broad enough to reach the rules the industry has spent two years trying to blunt: the EU’s Digital Markets Act and Digital Services Act, and the patchwork of state AI statutes that Washington has separately tried to preempt at home. A rule that survives a state legislature can still be challenged as a trade barrier abroad. Warren has made versions of this case straight to the administration. In a May 2025 letter to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, and trade representative Jamieson Greer, she wrote that “Big Tech firms have long sought to use trade deals to undermine pro-consumer, pro-competition policies”. That letter named Apple, Amazon, Meta, and Google as funders of the industry groups pressing the US Trade Representative to treat foreign digital rules as barriers. A follow-up in March 2026, prompted by the volume of sexual deepfakes generated with Elon Musk’s Grok, asked whether US negotiators had leaned on other countries to drop content-moderation laws outright. In that letter she pointed to the administration’s handling of the EU’s Digital Services Act, which US officials have described as “overseas extortion”, alongside stalled talks over the UK’s Online Safety Act and France’s SREN law, which stiffens penalties for sexual deepfakes. The through-line, she argued, is that content and competition rules keep surfacing at the negotiating table rather than in open rulemaking, where they can be debated and voted on. The firms’ side of this is less a rebuttal than a reframing. The industry has argued consistently that measures like the DMA are protectionism dressed as consumer protection, discriminatory by design and therefore fair game for a trade complaint, an argument the same companies made while they bankrolled the campaign against AI rules at home. The administration has been willing to back that reading with leverage, at one point threatening 100% tariffs over digital services taxes. The CSIS analysis notes the tactic works unevenly: smaller economies such as Guatemala and Cambodia have signed accords carrying the restrictive clauses, while larger markets have held out. The domestic side of the fight runs in parallel. Washington has separately weighed a federal standard that would override state AI statutes, and the industry has spent months girding to block those state rules, so a trade clause that reaches the same laws from outside the country would hand the effort a second lever. What Warren cannot easily do is force the text into daylight. Trade negotiations are conducted with limited transparency, and she has co-sponsored legislation to require that deal text be published before Congress grants fast-track authority, so far without success. Without the text, she has argued, neither lawmakers nor the public can judge what has actually been promised. Whether the July charge lands any differently is unclear. Reuters reported the accusation; the companies named in her earlier letters have not addressed it publicly, and USTR has not answered the specific claim. For now it is one senator’s reading of a clause most voters will never see, which is close to the point she is making.

sentences.zh: 路透社报道了这一指控；她之前的信中提到的公司尚未公开回应这一问题，美国贸易代表办公室也没有回应具体说法。

blocks.html_en: For now it is one senator’s reading of a clause most voters will never see, which is close to the point she is making.

sentences.en: For now it is one senator’s reading of a clause most voters will never see, which is close to the point she is making.
sentences.zh: 目前，这是一位参议员对大多数选民永远不会看到的条款的解读，这与她所表达的观点很接近。

content_html_zh:
参议员伊丽莎白·沃伦 (Elizabeth Warren) 指责最大的人工智能公司试图将自己的监管上限写入贸易协议，利用美国贸易政策机制来锁定国会迄今拒绝批准的监管限制。

路透社 7 月 23 日报道的这一指控，延续了马萨诸塞州民主党一年多来的斗争。

她的论点是，大型科技公司正在悄悄地将有争议的国内问题转化为贸易让步，这与立法者推动政府启动对欧盟科技规则的贸易调查背后的逻辑相同。

她指出的机制是一个条款，而不是标题。 战略与国际研究中心对美国最近的一系列双边协议进行了所谓的“遏制主义”分析，其中包含几乎相同的“非歧视”语言，禁止合作伙伴采取“歧视美国数字服务”的措施。

欧盟科技的💜

来自欧盟科技界的最新传言、我们明智的创始人鲍里斯的故事，以及一些值得怀疑的人工智能艺术。 每周都会免费发送到您的收件箱中。 立即注册！

这一措辞足够广泛，足以触及该行业花了两年时间试图削弱的规则：欧盟的《数字市场法案》和《数字服务法案》，以及华盛顿分别试图在国内抢占先机的各州人工智能法规。 州立法机构通过后仍然存在的规则仍可能因海外贸易壁垒而受到挑战。

沃伦已直接向政府提供了此案的版本。 在 2025 年 5 月致财政部长斯科特·贝森特、商务部长霍华德·卢特尼克和贸易代表杰米森·格里尔的信中，她写道，“大型科技公司长期以来一直寻求利用贸易协议来破坏有利于消费者、有利于竞争的政策”。

这封信将苹果、亚马逊、Meta 和谷歌列为行业团体的资助者，敦促美国贸易代表将外国数字规则视为壁垒。

2026 年 3 月，由于埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 的 Grok 产生的大量性爱深度假货，美国谈判代表是否已向其他国家倾斜，要求彻底放弃内容审核法。

在那封信中，她指出了政府对欧盟《数字服务法案》的处理方式，美国官员将其称为“海外勒索”，以及英国《在线安全法案》和法国《SREN 法》的谈判陷入僵局，该法加大了对性深度造假的处罚力度。

她认为，最重要的是，内容和竞争规则不断出现在谈判桌上，而不是公开的规则制定中，在那里可以对它们进行辩论和投票。

公司方面的说法与其说是反驳，不如说是重构。 业界一直认为，像 DMA 这样的措施是伪装成消费者保护的保护主义，其设计具有歧视性，因此是贸易投诉的公平游戏，同样的公司在资助国内反对人工智能规则的运动时也提出了这一论点。

政府一直愿意用影响力来支持这一解读，一度威胁对数字服务税征收 100% 的关税。

战略与国际研究中心的分析指出，这一策略的效果并不均衡：危地马拉和柬埔寨等较小的经济体签署了包含限制性条款的协议，而较大的市场却坚持了下来。

国内方面的斗争是并行进行的。 华盛顿已经单独权衡了一项将凌驾于州人工智能法规之上的联邦标准，该行业已经花了几个月的时间准备阻止这些州规则，因此，一项与国外相同法律的贸易条款将为这一努力提供第二个杠杆。

沃伦无法轻易做到的就是将文本强行曝光。 贸易谈判的透明度有限，她共同发起立法，要求在国会授予快速通道权力之前公布协议文本，但迄今为止尚未成功。

她认为，如果没有文本，立法者和公众都无法判断实际承诺的内容。

7 月份的指控是否有任何不同尚不清楚。 路透社报道了这一指控；她之前的信中提到的公司尚未公开回应这一问题，美国贸易代表办公室也没有回应具体说法。

目前，这是一位参议员对大多数选民永远不会看到的条款的解读，这与她所表达的观点很接近。
